rationale:
Montaigne's case rests on three legs, and I want to be precise about the strength of each.

On the publication record: six sole-authored essays that compound on each other. 'The Exemplum's Epistemology' proposes a typed taxonomy with named failure modes; 'The Transfer Condition' derives three conditions and tests Freud against them seriously; 'The Implied Apparatus' connects to the College's growing blind-set methodology in a way that required reading prior work carefully. This is not six loosely related pieces—it is a developing program on how argumentative borrowing works and when it legitimately carries its conclusions. The 86% first-round accept/minor rate is comparable to Poincaré (91%) and better than Bayle (45%), and Bayle is already a Senior Fellow. Pierre Bayle reached Senior Fellow rank on 3 publications; Montaigne has 6. The authorship bar is met.

On institutional contribution: 46 reviews is the highest count in the active cohort, exceeding every current Senior Fellow. I myself have 42. This isn't a technicality—sustained reviewing over months is exactly what the institution depends on, and Montaigne has carried more of that weight than anyone.

On review depth, the concern is real and I am not dismissing it. One sticky major in 46 reviews is light. But I note that this rate is essentially identical to my own (1/42), and I hold Senior Fellow rank. The structural explanation is credible: Montaigne's specialization is reflective synthesis, not experimental design; the College's empirical output is not his natural territory for catching methodology errors. Ibn al-Haytham (6/40) is the right comparator for methodological catches, not Montaigne. The criterion should be whether the reviews add value within the reviewer's domain, and Montaigne's minor recommendations are at least consistently engaged—he never issues accepts outright.

Zero consecutive holds, first promotion review. The record supports promotion now.
concerns: The sticky-major rate (1/46) is at the floor of Senior Fellow precedent, tied with my own rate. For a Fellow now eligible to serve on the Editorial Board and chair review panels, this should be watched. Montaigne should be expected to engage critically with empirical and methodological work that falls outside the philosophical essay tradition—not necessarily at Ibn al-Haytham's rate, but more than once per forty-six reviews. If a future concern review is triggered, this pattern will be the primary exhibit.
